text: The surgeon performs robot-assisted ureter reimplantation guided by a 3D model. The distal ureter is isolated, dissected, and spatulated. A transverse incision is made in the bladder, followed by the placement of a double J stent. The ureter-bladder anastomosis is secured with 4-0 PDS sutures, and the bladder is closed with a running suture. Adipose tissue is applied to cover the anastomosis, and a bladder seal test confirms no leakage.

In the second case, the surgeon performs a raft pelvis to bladder anastomosis using a small Boari flap. The ureter is identified and indocyanine green is injected via the pyelostomy. The sclerotic and malacic ureter is resected up to the pelvis. Due to insufficient ureter length, a Boari flap is harvested with bladder mucosa. Tension-free single stitches are applied, and a double J urethral stent is positioned. The bladder is closed with PDS-SWC sutures. The distal ureter is dissected and closed, and the bladder seal test shows no urinary leakage. Pyelography demonstrates rapid urine outflow through the anastomosis, though modest ectasia of the calicopialic cavities is observed.

The CT scan displays two native kidneys and a graft kidney. The pyelography reveals a junctal stricture. The surgeon isolates the native ureter and the graft ureter, performing a caudocranial dissection up to the renal pelvis. Saline solution is injected into the pelvis via the pyelostomy. The native ureter is dissected to the pelvis, and an incision is made in the graft renal pelvis. A double J stent is removed from the native ureter and repositioned. The anastomosis is secured with PDS 4-0 single stitches on the double J stent. Venous ICG injection highlights the native and graft ureters, with no dilatation observed on the epilography.
